findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Change the Implementation section's 'Tests assert…' from present tense to future-tense obligation: 'The new `tests/unit/test_compute_validity_callers.py` (AC) will prove that no current caller positionally unpacks or exact-shape validates a plan-review result — this must be written and pass BEFORE the additive `health` key is merged.'
evidence: Search for `test_compute_validity_callers` in `tests/` returned zero results
AC explicitly creates `tests/unit/test_compute_validity_callers.py` as new work
The Implementation section uses present tense ('Tests assert') implying this proof currently exists
findings.finding: The Implementation section states 'Tests assert no current caller positionally unpacks or exact-shape validates a plan-review result' as a present-tense justification for the additive `health` key being safe. But `tests/unit/test_compute_validity_callers.py` does NOT exist in the test suite (confirmed by search). The plan's AC creates this test — it is a NEW test file, not an existing proof. The present-tense claim in the Implementation ('Tests assert…') is an unbacked assertion about currently-existing test coverage that serves as the premise for the rollback claim ('no stored-data migration is required').
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Tests assert no current caller positionally unpacks or exact-shape validates a plan-review result'
scenarios: An implementer may skip creating the AST-scan test believing the proof already exists
If a current caller does positional-unpack (not caught until the new test is written), the additive health key silently breaks it

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the leaf's DerivedPlanReviewHealth definition to include `phase_status` (with a placeholder value such as `compatible` when phase data is not yet available) or explicitly document how the partial contract is safe for all current consumers until child 5967 ships, matching the parent's settled structure.
evidence: Parent (jira-reb-1160) Enforcement and rollout section: 'compute_validity preserves its top-level {valid, reason, verdict} contract and adds health: DerivedPlanReviewHealth. That nested contract has pin_status exactly current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, or legacy-unpinned; phase_status exactly compatible, incompatible, or malformed; enforced boolean; and targets: list[TargetPinDetail].'

Leaf (75de) Implementation: 'Own the nested DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract... It has pin_status exactly current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, or legacy-unpinned; enforced: bool; and targets: list[TargetPinDetail].' — phase_status is entirely absent from this definition.
The parent's settled design names phase_status as a sibling field to pin_status in the same structure, not as a later extension.
findings.finding: The leaf's DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract omits phase_status and frames child 5967 as 'extending' it, but the parent epic's settled design defines phase_status as a first-class field of DerivedPlanReviewHealth alongside pin_status, enforced, and targets — it is not an extension point.
findings.location: Implementation § DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract: 'Child 5967 extends the same contract with phase_status and phase compatibility after it introduces the phase manifest format.'
scenarios: Child 5967 ships after this story and attempts to 'extend' DerivedPlanReviewHealth with phase_status, but if this story shipped the contract without a phase_status slot, the interface change is additive rather than fulfilling the parent's original contract — leaving an interim period where compute_validity returns a health object that violates the parent's defined shape.

Any consumer (audit, UI, close gate) that reads DerivedPlanReviewHealth per the parent's contract and accesses health.phase_status would find the field absent until 5967 ships, despite the parent defining it as always-present.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) declare that this story ships DerivedPlanReviewHealth with phase_status always present but computed as a stub/default (e.g., phase_status='compatible' until child 5967 wires real phase logic), so the parent's contract is satisfied from day one; or (b) update the parent epic to explicitly designate phase_status as an extension point that child 5967 adds, so the parent's contract and leaf's contract are consistent — but update the parent first per the conflict rule.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (targeted at `tests/unit/test_attestation_invalidation.py` or a dedicated pin-health unit) that directly verifies mixed-severity multi-target aggregation: e.g., 'when one target is `current` and another is `stale-pin-drift`, `DerivedPlanReviewHealth.pin_status` equals `stale-pin-drift`; when one target is `stale-pin-drift` and another is `malformed-pin`, aggregate equals `malformed-pin`.'
evidence: 'Aggregate target status by fixed severity malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current' — stated in DerivedPlanReviewHealth description
'Aggregate in this helper by malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current' — restated in derive_plan_material_pin_health description
ABSENCE: no AC reads 'when target A is stale-pin-drift and target B is current, health.pin_status equals stale-pin-drift' or equivalent mixed-severity assertion
findings.finding: The Implementation describes a multi-target severity aggregation rule — 'Aggregate in this helper by malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current' — as a deliverable behavior of `derive_plan_material_pin_health`, but no Acceptance Criterion directly verifies that when multiple targets have different per-target statuses the aggregate `DerivedPlanReviewHealth.pin_status` reflects the highest-severity target. The individual target-status ACs (stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, etc.) only cover single-target cases or top-level outcomes; none explicitly exercises mixed-severity aggregation.
findings.location: Implementation: 'Aggregate in this helper by malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current'; 'Aggregate target status by fixed severity malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current' — both under 'derive_plan_material_pin_health' and 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract' sections. No corresponding AC.
scenarios: An implementer returns the first target's status or the last target's status rather than the max-severity target's status; no AC catches the regression.
A future refactor changes the aggregation order; no AC protects the contract.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '`derive_plan_material_pin_health` aggregates multi-target status by severity order `malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current`; `tests/unit/test_attestation_invalidation.py` directly proves mixed-severity inputs yield the highest-severity aggregate `pin_status`.'

evidence: 31 acceptance-criteria items (> 25)
findings.finding: Oversize signals suggest this unit may be too large for one session.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into smaller child tickets, each a coherent single outcome.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the AC for `sidecar.parse_reviewed_related_material` with the Implementation's explicit raise/catch pattern: either rewrite the AC to say 'raises `ReviewedRelatedMaterialError`; callers must catch it at their own boundary' or rewrite the Implementation to say the function returns a typed error value — pick one contract and state it consistently in both sections.
evidence: AC phrase: 'maps every present-invalid variant to malformed/invalid without raising past a consumer boundary'
Implementation phrase: 'raises ReviewedRelatedMaterialError for any present invalid list/item/key/role/ID/fingerprint/order/duplicate'

The phrase 'without raising past a consumer boundary' intends to describe a catch-at-boundary pattern, but the AC does not make this explicit — leaving it ambiguous whether the raise is internal-only or prohibited entirely
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criterion for `sidecar.parse_reviewed_related_material` says it maps present-invalid inputs to 'malformed/invalid without raising past a consumer boundary,' which an implementer could reasonably read as: the function should NOT raise at all. The Implementation body explicitly states the opposite — 'raises ReviewedRelatedMaterialError for any present invalid list/item/key/role/ID/fingerprint/order/duplicate' — and relies on callers (re-sign, compute_validity) catching that specific error to produce the malformed-pin outcome. These two descriptions describe the same architecture differently enough to send an implementer in two directions on the function's return/raise contract.
findings.location: AC: 'sidecar.parse_reviewed_related_material returns None only for an absent field and maps every present-invalid variant to malformed/invalid without raising past a consumer boundary.' Implementation: 'raises ReviewedRelatedMaterialError for any present invalid list/item/key/role/ID/fingerprint/order/duplicate. Re-sign catches that exact error and returns malformed-pin without raising.'
scenarios: An implementer reads the AC literally and implements parse_reviewed_related_material to return a sentinel/malformed value rather than raise, breaking re-sign's catch-specific-error logic and the 'Malformed pin syntax returns malformed-pin immediately' path in compute_validity.
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite the AC to: '`sidecar.parse_reviewed_related_material` returns `None` only for an absent field and raises `ReviewedRelatedMaterialError` for any present-invalid input; callers (`re-sign`, `compute_validity`) catch this error at their own boundary and produce the `malformed-pin` outcome without re-raising.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the `failure_kind` three-value vocabulary (`resolution`, `io`, `reducer`) with the `plan_material_pin_target_unreadable` warning event schema and enumerate the concrete exception/error types that map to each category.
evidence: Plan quote: 'Canonical lookup ambiguity/mismatch maps to failure_kind=resolution; tracker/path/read/permission/OSError maps to io; reducer or narrow-fingerprint computation failure maps to reducer. Each non-absence failure emits structured warning plan_material_pin_target_unreadable'
The mapping appears only once in prose; no warning-event schema or helper contract table co-locates the three values with their triggering exception/error types

Existing codebase uses failure_kind elsewhere (src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/health.py:32) with a distinct two-value vocabulary ('apply_error', 'reschedule'), confirming the pattern requires explicit co-location
findings.finding: `failure_kind` three-value vocabulary (`resolution`, `io`, `reducer`) for the `plan_material_pin_target_unreadable` structured warning is named only once in a prose paragraph inside the `derive_plan_material_pin_health` description, but is never co-located with either (a) the warning event's schema definition or (b) the helper's formal contract. An executor implementing the emission logic must search the prose to discover which exception types map to each category.
findings.location: Implementation § derive_plan_material_pin_health — 'Canonical lookup ambiguity/mismatch maps to failure_kind=resolution; tracker/path/read/permission/OSError maps to io; reducer or narrow-fingerprint computation failure maps to reducer'
scenarios: An executor implementing the io branch catches OSError but not PermissionError (or vice versa) because the exception-type boundary is not specified

A 'resolution' failure for ambiguous canonical lookup is implemented differently by different executors if the triggering condition (ambiguity vs. mismatch vs. both) is not formally defined next to the enum
findings.suggested_fix: Add a small table or typed dataclass alongside `derive_plan_material_pin_health`'s contract definition: `resolution` = any `LookupError`/canonical-ID ambiguity from the tracker resolver; `io` = `OSError`, `PermissionError`, and ticket-read errors; `reducer` = any exception from the narrow-material reducer or fingerprint computation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly whether `compute_validity` calls `sidecar.parse_reviewed_related_material` internally (and catches `ReviewedRelatedMaterialError` itself) or receives already-parsed `Sequence[PlanMaterialPin] | None` from its caller, and document the catch boundary for each call site.
evidence: Plan quote on ordering: 'After signature certification and plan-review-kind validation, parse pins and build DerivedPlanReviewHealth before all reopen, registry, code, and own-material early returns'
Plan quote on re-sign: 'Re-sign catches that exact error and returns malformed-pin without raising'

compute_validity's current signature takes `attestation, ticket_state, kind` — no pre-parsed PlanMaterialPin sequence; yet derive_plan_material_pin_health takes `pin_records: Sequence[PlanMaterialPin] | None`
The catch site for ReviewedRelatedMaterialError inside compute_validity (vs. a wrapper) is unspecified
findings.finding: The decision rule governing where `sidecar.parse_reviewed_related_material` (which raises `ReviewedRelatedMaterialError`) is called vs. where `derive_plan_material_pin_health` (which accepts already-parsed `Sequence[PlanMaterialPin] | None`) is invoked inside `compute_validity` is not specified. The plan says re-sign 'catches that exact error and returns malformed-pin without raising', but for the `compute_validity` path the parse-then-catch chain placement is left unspecified — the plan only says 'parse pins and build DerivedPlanReviewHealth before all reopen, registry, code, and own-material early returns', without stating whether `compute_validity` calls `parse_reviewed_related_material` itself or receives already-parsed records from its caller.
findings.location: Implementation § compute_validity ordering — 'After signature certification and plan-review-kind validation, parse pins and build DerivedPlanReviewHealth before all reopen...' vs. § sidecar.parse_reviewed_related_material — 'raises ReviewedRelatedMaterialError for any present invalid list/item/key/role/ID/fingerprint/order/duplicate. Re-sign catches that exact error'
scenarios: One executor places the parse+catch inside compute_validity; another places it in a pre-call wrapper in drift_refresh_candidate; the two produce different error propagation behavior on malformed sidecars

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback config-file state: either confirm the config loader silently ignores unknown keys (so `enforce_plan_material_pins` in existing files is harmless after rollback), or add a rollback step that removes the key from user config files, or document that users must remove the key before rolling back.
evidence: 'Rollback removes the additive health key and its consumers; no stored-data migration is required.' — asserts sidecar rollback but is silent on config-file rollback.
The plan does NOT assert whether the rolled-back config schema will accept or reject existing config files containing the new key.
T11 requires the plan to assert the schema STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0 or needs no migration.
findings.finding: The plan asserts rollback safety for sidecar files ('pre-feature v2 readers ignore it') but does NOT assert the post-rollback schema state for config files that already contain `enforce_plan_material_pins: true`. If the config loader rejects unknown keys under strict-schema parsing, any deployment that wrote this key to its config before rollback will encounter a `ConfigError` on every config load after rollback — potentially breaking all attestation operations.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Register `enforce_plan_material_pins: bool = False` on `_config_schema.py::VerifyConfig`' and 'Rollback removes the additive health key and its consumers; no stored-data migration is required.'
scenarios: A user sets `enforce_plan_material_pins: true` in their config file, then the feature is rolled back. The rolled-back schema no longer registers the key. If strict-schema parsing rejects unknown keys, every subsequent config load raises `ConfigError`, breaking claim-gate, compute_validity, and all attestation consumers.
Even if the config loader is lenient, the plan gives no explicit guarantee — a future schema-strictness change could silently introduce breakage.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the rollback description: 'The config loader treats unknown `verify.*` keys as silently ignored; config files containing `enforce_plan_material_pins` after rollback will not cause a `ConfigError`. Users need not modify their config to roll back.' If the loader is NOT lenient, add an explicit rollback step: 'Remove `enforce_plan_material_pins` from all config files before rolling back the code.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact ordering rule for `reviewed_related_material` items in `build_payload` serialization (e.g., stable-insertion order / ascending `canonical_id` / role-then-canonical_id) and confirm `parse_reviewed_related_material` validates that same rule.
evidence: '`build_payload` serializes it as an ordered list of exact {role, canonical_id, material_fingerprint} items'
'raises `ReviewedRelatedMaterialError` for any present invalid list/item/key/role/ID/fingerprint/order/duplicate'
No ordering key or ordering rule is stated anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `build_payload` serializes `reviewed_related_material` as 'an ordered list' and that `parse_reviewed_related_material` raises `ReviewedRelatedMaterialError` for invalid 'order', but never defines what ordering rule is enforced (e.g., ascending by `canonical_id`, role-then-id, insertion order). An executor cannot implement the serializer or the validator, and cannot write a test that distinguishes valid order from invalid order.
findings.location: Implementation bullet: 'sidecar.parse_reviewed_related_material … raises ReviewedRelatedMaterialError for any present invalid list/item/key/role/ID/fingerprint/order/duplicate'
scenarios: Executor implements ascending-by-canonical_id order; test author assumes role-first order; tests fail or silently pass the wrong invariant.
A legitimate sidecar with a different-but-valid ordering is incorrectly rejected as malformed on read.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence such as: 'Items are serialized in stable ascending order by `(role, canonical_id)`. `parse_reviewed_related_material` raises `ReviewedRelatedMaterialError` if items are not in this order.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each of ACs #14–#17 and #20, name the specific test file (existing or new) and the test-case description that proves the criterion, and add those files to the `pytest` command in the Verification section.
evidence: Verification section lists: 'test_attestation_invalidation.py test_plan_review_resign.py test_plan_review_sidecar.py test_drift_refresh_candidate.py test_plan_material_pin_config.py test_compute_validity_callers.py'
AC #14: no '`tests/unit/test_…`' pointer given
AC #15: no test pointer given
AC #16: no test pointer given
AC #17: no test pointer given
AC #20: no test pointer given
findings.finding: Acceptance criteria #14 (missing/deleted/unreadable/reducer-failing targets → `stale-pin-missing`; non-missing read failures emit `plan_material_pin_target_unreadable`), #15 (archived targets compare normally), #16 (duplicate role-plus-ID pins → `malformed-pin`), #17 (re-enabling enforcement immediately invalidates drifted pins), and #20 (`parse_reviewed_related_material` returns `None` only for absent field and maps every present-invalid variant to malformed/invalid) have no named test file in the verification section or in the ACs themselves. No proving command is mapped to these criteria.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria #14–#17 and #20; Verification section
scenarios: An executor writes code satisfying these behaviors but adds no tests because none were required, and CI does not catch regressions.
A code reviewer cannot verify these behaviors are tested without searching every file.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a new `tests/unit/test_plan_material_pin_health.py` covering: (14) target read-failure modes, (15) archived-target comparison, (16) duplicate-pin detection, (17) enforcement-toggle invalidation. For AC #20 add a `test_sidecar_parse_reviewed_related_material.py` section or extend `test_plan_review_sidecar.py` with explicit parse-validation cases and reference it in the verification command.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit test (in a named test file, e.g. `test_plan_review_run.py` or within `test_plan_review_sidecar.py`) that mocks `sidecar.emit` and asserts it receives the exact `review_snapshot.related_material` value captured before the LLM call, and reference this test in the Verification section.
evidence: '`tests/unit/test_plan_review_sidecar.py` proves a pre-feature sidecar reader ignores additive `reviewed_related_material` and otherwise behaves identically' — this is the only test named in this AC group
No test named for the call-site threading assertion in `_run_plan_review`
findings.finding: AC #7 states 'Full LLM review threads the exact pre-LLM `review_snapshot.related_material` through `sidecar.emit` and `build_payload`' but only `test_plan_review_sidecar.py` is named in the ACs for that bullet — and that test is scoped to proving 'a pre-feature sidecar reader ignores additive `reviewed_related_material` and otherwise behaves identically'. No named test proves that `_run_plan_review` actually passes `review_snapshot.related_material` (not a stale copy, None, or a re-fetched snapshot) to `sidecar.emit`.
findings.location: Acceptance Criterion #7: 'Full LLM review threads the exact pre-LLM review_snapshot.related_material through sidecar.emit and build_payload'
scenarios: Implementation accidentally passes `None` or a post-LLM re-fetch; tests remain green because `test_plan_review_sidecar.py` only checks read-side compatibility, not write-side call arguments.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md section capturing (a) the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract as the canonical validity sub-contract, (b) the PlanValidityProfile 'distinct call-site intent' invariant and its rationale, and (c) the key-local fail-open `_read_enforce_plan_material_pins` pattern as the project convention for optional enforcement flags.
evidence: Plan defines DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract shape inline with no pointer to a captured decision record.

'Define PlanValidityProfile with exactly DEFAULT, CLOSE, and DRIFT_REFRESH' — enum invariant ('CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH deliberately share one rule ... so later close policy cannot silently change progressive refresh') is purely prose; no ADR.
_read_enforce_plan_material_pins is 'modeled on mcp_readonly()' — the pattern is identified but not documented as a project-wide convention for future reuse.
No mention of AGENTS.md, ADR, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan introduces three novel architectural decisions — (1) the DerivedPlanReviewHealth sub-contract on compute_validity results, (2) the PlanValidityProfile dispatch enum with its 'shared rule, distinct call-site intent' invariant, and (3) the key-local fail-open _read_enforce_plan_material_pins pattern — but names no ADR, AGENTS.md entry, or design-doc update to capture them.
findings.location: Implementation section — paragraphs on DerivedPlanReviewHealth, PlanValidityProfile, and _read_enforce_plan_material_pins
scenarios: A future contributor wires a new profile without understanding the 'distinct call-site intent' invariant and conflates CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH, silently breaking the progressive-refresh isolation the plan explicitly cares about.
A contributor implementing a new config key doesn't know the fail-open key-local pattern exists and duplicates it inconsistently or uses the general verify.* posture instead.
Child cb6b picks up DerivedPlanReviewHealth for UI rendering but has to reverse-engineer the contract from code rather than a canonical spec.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR (or AGENTS.md block) titled 'Plan-material pin health and validity profiles' that records: the DerivedPlanReviewHealth field contract, the rule that CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH must remain distinct call sites even when their policy overlaps, and the key-local fail-open pattern as the canonical way to add optional enforcement flags so future keys follow the same boundary.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete motivation section: state the current behavior gap (e.g., 'today, compute_validity does not check related-material pin drift, so a drifted dependency is incorrectly treated as certified'), or reference a parent epic finding / failing test that reproduces the problem.
evidence: Goal states: 'Use signed pins as the single related-material validity check across every attestation lifecycle consumer' — asserts a desired state but never shows the current behavior fails this
No reproduction steps, no failing test, no Expected/Actual anywhere in the plan
No named defect: the plan never states 'currently, when X happens, Y incorrectly results'
References to children (acaf, 5967, 2bbd, cb6b) suggest a parent epic may hold the motivation, but this plan's own text contains none
findings.finding: The plan proposes significant new mechanisms (DerivedPlanReviewHealth, enforce_plan_material_pins config, derive_plan_material_pin_health, sidecar parsing, drift_refresh_candidate refactor, compute_validity extension, resign extension) but never demonstrates that current behavior is wrong or insufficient — there is no reproduction, no Expected-vs-Actual, no named defect, and no stated user/system problem the current behavior causes.
findings.location: Goal and Implementation sections
scenarios: A reviewer cannot determine whether the current attestation lifecycle already enforces related-material validity in some form, making it impossible to assess whether the added mechanism is additive, redundant, or corrective
Without a stated gap, the enforcement flag default (false) and its rollout rationale cannot be evaluated against the actual problem being solved
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Current Behavior / Gap' subsection before Implementation: describe what compute_validity currently does with related-material pins (likely: nothing, because child acaf introduces pins), cite the attestation lifecycle consumer(s) that would silently pass drifted material, and state the user/system impact (e.g., a plan review attested against stale dependencies can be reused without re-review).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Defer `PlanValidityProfile.CLOSE` to child 2bbd (the story that actually wires it), so this story ships only `DEFAULT` and `DRIFT_REFRESH`, each with a live call-site.
evidence: Plan explicitly: 'child 2bbd later wires `CLOSE` into `transition_close`' — CLOSE has zero active call-sites at this story's ship boundary.
AC: 'This story selects `DRIFT_REFRESH`; child 2bbd separately owns selecting `CLOSE` at close call sites.'
Codebase: no PlanValidityProfile symbol exists yet (grep returned zero src/ hits); the enum is net-new and CLOSE would ship unused.
Plan: 'CLOSE and DRIFT_REFRESH deliberately share one rule in this story — disable only code freshness' — identical runtime behavior at ship.
findings.finding: `PlanValidityProfile.CLOSE` is defined in this story but has zero wired call-sites at ship: its only named consumer (`transition_close`) is explicitly deferred to child 2bbd. An enum value with no active wiring at this story's boundary is YAGNI dead-code and violates the Rule-of-Three for non-trivial abstractions that gate behavior.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 5: 'Define `PlanValidityProfile` with exactly `DEFAULT`, `CLOSE`, and `DRIFT_REFRESH`… child 2bbd later wires `CLOSE` into `transition_close`'
scenarios: If child 2bbd is cancelled or reordered, CLOSE permanently ships as dead code.
An implementor seeing two identically-behaved values may collapse them, breaking the planned call-site separation later.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `CLOSE` from the enum in this story's scope. Child 2bbd adds it when it wires `transition_close`. The plan already says 'child 2bbd later wires `CLOSE`' — just enforce that boundary by not shipping the value here.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `_read_enforce_plan_material_pins` is justified as a shared boundary (multiple diverging consumers) or whether the existing inline `load_config(repo_root).verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` pattern suffices; if only one read-site exists, use the inline pattern and drop the bespoke function.
evidence: Codebase pattern: `load_config(repo_root).verify.overlap_enabled` (enrich_drain.py:267), `load_config(repo_root).verify` (drift_floor.py:171, 306), `load_config(repo_root).verify.enable_code_review` (gate_dispatch.py:447) — all read VerifyConfig flags inline without a bespoke function.
mcp_readonly() is its own function because MCP server AND LLM runner both read it, each with diverging fail-closed/fail-open semantics — the plan's own docstring says so (config.py:402-414).
For `enforce_plan_material_pins`, the plan names a single primary reader (`derive_plan_material_pin_health` → `compute_validity` and `resign_plan_review`), not two diverging consumers.
Introducing a named boundary for one flag not shared across divergent call-sites is a premature abstraction compared to the existing inline pattern.
findings.finding: `_read_enforce_plan_material_pins` is a bespoke per-key reader function wrapping a single VerifyConfig attribute access. The codebase pattern for VerifyConfig flags is direct inline reads (`load_config(repo_root).verify.<flag>`) — this pattern is used in 10+ sites across `plan_review/`. The plan cites `mcp_readonly()` as the model, but `mcp_readonly()` is justified because it is shared across two diverging call-sites with opposite fail-closed semantics; a single-consumer enforcement flag does not meet that justification.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 1: 'Add `_read_enforce_plan_material_pins(repo_root) -> bool` as the only read boundary for this optional key'
scenarios: Future readers searching for where `enforce_plan_material_pins` is consumed find an indirection that obscures the direct config → flag path.
The bespoke function adds test surface (test_plan_material_pin_config.py is named specifically for it) for behavior already covered by the shared ConfigError-catch pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'retain child acaf's pre-LLM `PlanRelationSnapshot` as local `review_snapshot`' with an explicit cross-story dependency statement: 'Depends on child acaf shipping `PlanRelationSnapshot` first; this story reads `review_snapshot.related_material` only after acaf's type and field are confirmed in `_run_plan_review`'.
evidence: Searched `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py` lines 489–640 for `review_snapshot` — no matches
Searched entire repo for `PlanRelationSnapshot` — zero results in `src/`
Searched entire repo for `review_snapshot` in `src/` — no matches
'retain' implies the symbol already exists in `_run_plan_review`, which is false
findings.finding: The plan says 'In `_run_plan_review`, retain child acaf's pre-LLM `PlanRelationSnapshot` as local `review_snapshot`' — but `_run_plan_review` (verified at `__init__.py:489–640`) contains no `review_snapshot` variable and no `PlanRelationSnapshot` type anywhere in the `plan_review/` package. The verb 'retain' asserts this is an already-present codebase artifact; it is not. `PlanRelationSnapshot` is a deliverable of sibling story acaf, which must ship first. If this story executes before acaf, the `sidecar.emit(..., reviewed_related_material=review_snapshot.related_material)` call will produce a `NameError` at runtime.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'In `_run_plan_review`, retain child acaf's pre-LLM `PlanRelationSnapshot` as local `review_snapshot`'
scenarios: If this story ships before child acaf, `review_snapshot.related_material` is a NameError on the sidecar emit path
If child acaf changes the field name from `related_material`, this plan's hardcoded attribute reference breaks silently
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit ordering constraint: 'This story REQUIRES child acaf to have already landed `PlanRelationSnapshot` in `_run_plan_review`; the `review_snapshot` local is introduced by acaf, not retained from the current codebase.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/golden/config_surface.json` as an explicit edit target (add `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` entry) so CI `test_config_surface_golden.py` does not fail.
evidence: tests/golden/config_surface.json exists and lists all verify.* keys (lines 31–45); `enforce_plan_material_pins` is absent
tests/unit/test_config_surface_golden.py is an anti-regression guard that will fail if a new verify.* key is added without updating the golden file
The plan names `_config_schema.py` and `_SECTIONS['verify']` as edit targets but omits `tests/golden/config_surface.json`
findings.finding: `tests/golden/config_surface.json` is an implicit, required edit target that the plan never explicitly lists. Adding `enforce_plan_material_pins` to `_SECTIONS['verify']` / `VerifyConfig` will cause `tests/unit/test_config_surface_golden.py` to fail CI unless the golden file is updated. The plan says 'the config-surface golden records the key' but treats this as automatic rather than as a named edit target that must be deliberately updated.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Register `enforce_plan_material_pins: bool = False` on `_config_schema.py::VerifyConfig`… the config-surface golden records the key'
scenarios: CI test_config_surface_golden.py fails because verify.enforce_plan_material_pins is present in _SECTIONS but absent from the golden file
make check fails on a plan that otherwise correctly implements all other ACs
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"verify.enforce_plan_material_pins"` to the `config_keys` array in `tests/golden/config_surface.json` and list `tests/golden/config_surface.json` in the plan's file_impact / edit targets.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py` as an explicit edit target covering the `_run_plan_review` sidecar threading change and the `_idempotent_reuse` → `profile=DEFAULT` explicit call.
evidence: `_run_plan_review` is defined at `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:489`
`_idempotent_reuse` is defined at `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:438`
The plan's Implementation names both functions as edit targets but never lists `__init__.py` as a file to touch
The plan's Verification `pytest` command does not include any test directly covering `__init__.py` edits
findings.finding: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py` is a required edit target not named in the plan. It hosts `_run_plan_review` (which the plan directs to 'retain child acaf's pre-LLM PlanRelationSnapshot as local `review_snapshot`' and to call `sidecar.emit(..., reviewed_related_material=review_snapshot.related_material)`) and `_idempotent_reuse` (which the plan says must 'explicitly select `profile=PlanValidityProfile.DEFAULT`'). Both are substantive edits, but the file is not enumerated.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'In `_run_plan_review`, retain child acaf's pre-LLM `PlanRelationSnapshot` as local `review_snapshot`…' and 'Keep the existing claim-gate → `compute_validity` and idempotent-reuse → claim-gate routing, and make their call chain explicitly select `profile=PlanValidityProfile.DEFAULT`'
scenarios: Implementer edits `attest.py` and `sidecar.py` but does not update `_run_plan_review` in `__init__.py`, breaking the sidecar threading AC ('Full LLM review threads the exact pre-LLM review_snapshot.related_material through sidecar.emit')
The `profile=DEFAULT` explicit selection on the idempotent-reuse call chain is missed because `__init__.py` is not in scope

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale for returning `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` inline as an additive key on the `compute_validity` result (e.g., 'inline ensures every plan-review validity consumer automatically receives health with no additional call, preserving the single I/O round and preventing callers from bypassing pin health by calling a separate function').
evidence: Plan states 'Adding health to plan-review results is an additive internal interface change' without explaining why inline is preferred.
Plan states 'Advisory health is returned in-band; child cb6b separately owns audit and UI surfacing' without rationale for the in-band design.
A separate `get_plan_pin_health()` callable would allow callers to opt in — the plan never addresses why opt-in would be inferior for this use case.
G6 criterion: 'The plan states a POSITIVE rationale for the chosen approach (its absence is a finding).'
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for returning `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` inline as an additive key on the `compute_validity` result dict rather than as a separate callable (e.g., `get_plan_pin_health(attestation, ticket_state, *, repo_root, enforced)`). The implicit justification — consistent coverage of all plan-review consumers, single I/O round, guaranteed ordering — is never made explicit, leaving the architectural choice unexplained.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Adding health to plan-review results is an additive internal interface change.' and 'Own the nested DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract returned as health beside the existing attest.compute_validity top-level {valid, reason, verdict}'
scenarios: Future implementers add a new compute_validity consumer and don't realize they're automatically getting (and must handle) the health key, because the rationale for the inline design is not documented.
A reviewer questions why health is inline rather than a separate callable and the plan provides no answer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the severity-ordered aggregation rule (`malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current`) with the `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` schema definition, and explicitly state in the schema where `legacy-unpinned` may appear (top-level only, never in `TargetPinDetail.pin_status`).
evidence: Schema definition bullet: 'It has pin_status exactly current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, or legacy-unpinned; enforced: bool; and targets: list[TargetPinDetail]. Aggregate target status by fixed severity malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current'
Aggregation rule restated in derive_plan_material_pin_health bullet but not formally bound to the schema in one place
The rule for when legacy-unpinned applies at the top level vs. being forbidden at the target level is also split across two bullets
findings.finding: The aggregation rule reducing `targets: list[TargetPinDetail]` into the single top-level `DerivedPlanReviewHealth.pin_status` (fixed severity order: `malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current`) is stated in a prose fragment inside `derive_plan_material_pin_health`'s description, but is not co-located with either the `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` schema definition or the `TargetPinDetail` schema. The two schemas appear earlier in the Implementation bullet, and the rule appears much later — a reader of the schema definition alone cannot discover the aggregation protocol.
findings.location: Implementation § DerivedPlanReviewHealth schema definition ('pin_status exactly current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, or legacy-unpinned') vs. § derive_plan_material_pin_health ('Aggregate in this helper by malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current')
scenarios: An implementor reading only the DerivedPlanReviewHealth schema definition does not know how to compute the top-level pin_status from the targets list
A later extender of the schema (e.g. child 5967 adding phase_status) may not know the aggregation rule applies to both dimensions

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit self-contained field table for `PlanMaterialPin` in this plan's Implementation section (field names, types, and role enum values), rather than delegating the full contract definition to sibling child acaf.
evidence: Plan quote: 'PlanMaterialPin is child acaf's typed {role, canonical_id, material_fingerprint} record, and its material_fingerprint becomes TargetPinDetail.pinned_fingerprint'
No in-plan definition of field types (str? enum?), constraints (role must be 'child'|'prerequisite'?), or nullability is given for PlanMaterialPin
Code search for PlanMaterialPin returns zero matches in src/, confirming it does not yet exist and cannot be tool-verified
findings.finding: `PlanMaterialPin`'s complete field vocabulary is never formally defined within this plan — the plan says it is 'child acaf's typed `{role, canonical_id, material_fingerprint}` record'. The field names are partially inferrable from prose fragments ('its `material_fingerprint` becomes `TargetPinDetail.pinned_fingerprint`') but child acaf is a sibling deliverable, not an already-present import. An executor implementing `derive_plan_material_pin_health` in isolation from child acaf's output cannot confirm field names, types, or constraints from this plan alone.
findings.location: Implementation § derive_plan_material_pin_health — 'PlanMaterialPin is child acaf's typed {role, canonical_id, material_fingerprint} record'
scenarios: An executor implementing sidecar.parse_reviewed_related_material needs to know PlanMaterialPin's field contract to produce correctly typed outputs, but must infer it from prose scattered across two plans
findings.suggested_fix: Define inline: `PlanMaterialPin = {role: Literal['child', 'prerequisite'], canonical_id: str, material_fingerprint: str}` — the same shape already implied by TargetPinDetail and sidecar serialization.

findings.finding: The plan extends the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar with `reviewed_related_material` (the pre-LLM `PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material`) but does not specify the mechanism for threading this data from its collection point into `sidecar.emit` / `build_payload`. The current `sidecar.emit(verdict, material=material, repo_root=repo_root)` call in `_run_plan_review` only receives the post-LLM verdict and material fingerprint. The `related_material` records are collected before the LLM by child acaf in a separate code path; without an explicit threading mechanism (e.g., embedding under a reserved key in the verdict dict, or adding a `related_material=` parameter to `sidecar.emit`), `resign_plan_review` will not find `reviewed_related_material` in the sidecar payload it reads back, making the sidecar-based re-sign recovery unimplementable as specified.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Extend the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar payload with the exact review-time PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material records already collected before the LLM by child acaf'; also sidecar.py `emit` / `build_payload` / `__init__.py` `_run_plan_review`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `reviewed_related_material: list | None = None` parameter to `sidecar.emit` and `build_payload`, emit it as the serialized `reviewed_related_material` field. In `_run_plan_review`, thread the `PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material` (obtained from child acaf's collection step) into the `sidecar.emit(verdict, material=material, related_material=snapshot.related_material, repo_root=repo_root)` call. For the drift_refresh path, pass `None` (legacy-unpinned behavior) since drift-refresh re-signs without re-running the LLM.

findings.finding: `failure_kind` values are named (`resolution`, `io`, `reducer`) but the plan never specifies which exception or error types map to each category. An executor implementing `derive_plan_material_pin_health` must decide whether, e.g., a `FileNotFoundError` is `io` or `resolution`, whether a hash-computation error is `reducer` or `io`, and whether a registry lookup failure is `resolution` or `io`. Different choices produce different structured warning payloads and break the AC for `plan_material_pin_target_unreadable`.
findings.location: Implementation section: '`derive_plan_material_pin_health`' bullet — 'Every non-absence failure emits structured warning event `plan_material_pin_target_unreadable` with `failure_kind` exactly `resolution`, `io`, or `reducer`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the `derive_plan_material_pin_health` bullet: 'Map `failure_kind` as follows: path-not-found or permission errors → `io`; registry/canonical-ID resolution failures (target not in registry) → `resolution`; fingerprint computation or reducer failures → `reducer`.'

findings.finding: The `failure_kind` three-value vocabulary for the `plan_material_pin_target_unreadable` warning event (`resolution`, `io`, `reducer`) is named only once in a prose sentence. No rule is co-located with the `derive_plan_material_pin_health` spec that maps specific exception or error types to each category. An executor implementing the helper cannot determine which exceptions are `resolution` failures vs. `io` failures vs. `reducer` failures.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Every non-absence failure emits structured warning event `plan_material_pin_target_unreadable` with `failure_kind` exactly `resolution`, `io`, or `reducer`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table or sentence such as: 'resolution — the target canonical_id cannot be resolved to a store path (missing/deleted); io — the target path resolves but the file read raises OSError or equivalent; reducer — the fingerprint computation raises after a successful read.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly confirm in the `drift_refresh_candidate` refactoring description that the function's return shape `{manifest, deps, key_id}` is preserved (extracted from the verified attestation after `compute_validity(DRIFT_REFRESH)` succeeds), so the downstream `orchestrator.drift_refresh` consumer is not broken.
evidence: Current `orchestrator.drift_refresh` (line 596-601): `cand = attest.drift_refresh_candidate(...); current = attest._rehash(cand['deps'].keys()); drifted = {p for p, h in cand['deps'].items() ...}`
Current `orchestrator.drift_refresh` (line 645): `sig = attest.refresh_attestation(ctx.ticket_id, cand['manifest'], probe='PASS', ...)`

Plan says: 'After a valid result, retain only dependency extraction/nonempty validation and the current-versus-signed dependency-hash comparison' — does not explicitly say the return dict shape {manifest, deps, key_id} is preserved.

The plan says compute_validity result is evaluated ('an invalid result returns None before the probe') but doesn't say the manifest/deps/key_id still populate the return value after the compute_validity call succeeds.
findings.finding: The plan refactors `drift_refresh_candidate` to delegate to `compute_validity(DRIFT_REFRESH)` and states 'retain only dependency extraction/nonempty validation and the current-versus-signed dependency-hash comparison,' but never explicitly confirms that the function's return shape `{manifest, deps, key_id}` is preserved. The downstream `orchestrator.drift_refresh` consumes all three keys (`cand['manifest']`, `cand['deps']`, `cand['key_id']`); if an implementer treats the delegation as replacing rather than augmenting the return-value logic, the downstream consumer breaks silently.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'In `drift_refresh_candidate`, call `_reads.show_ticket` ... Remove the redundant inline signature, kind, registry, and own-material guards ... retain only dependency extraction/nonempty validation and the current-versus-signed dependency-hash comparison'
scenarios: Implementer refactors drift_refresh_candidate to return the compute_validity result dict instead of {manifest, deps, key_id}, breaking orchestrator.drift_refresh with a KeyError on cand['manifest'].
Implementer correctly infers the intent but without explicit confirmation, the return contract is ambiguous across code review.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the implementation paragraph: 'The function continues to return `{manifest, deps, key_id}` extracted from the verified attestation record; `compute_validity(DRIFT_REFRESH)` gates the return but does not replace it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Broaden the exception catch in `_read_enforce_plan_material_pins` to cover all exceptions (e.g., `except Exception`) so the fail-open boundary holds unconditionally, logging unexpected types at warning level with the same `plan_material_pin_config_unreadable` event and a distinguishing `exc_type` field.
evidence: Plan says the function 'catches `ConfigError`' only — no broader exception clause is stated.
The function is explicitly modeled as the 'only read boundary for this optional key' with a 'key-local fail-open rollout policy modeled on `mcp_readonly()`'.
ABSENCE: no `except Exception` or equivalent broad guard is specified, so any non-ConfigError exception propagates.
findings.finding: `_read_enforce_plan_material_pins` is described as a never-raising fail-open boundary but only catches `ConfigError`. If `load_config()` or attribute traversal raises any other exception type (e.g., `AttributeError`, `TypeError`, `ImportError`, unexpected `OSError`), the exception propagates to callers that expect this boundary to be unconditionally safe, breaking whatever attestation consumer invoked it.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 1: '`_read_enforce_plan_material_pins(repo_root) -> bool` … catches `ConfigError`, emits a structured `logger.warning` … and returns false'
scenarios: A Python environment issue causes `load_config()` to raise `ImportError` — the attestation consumer propagates the error instead of failing open.

A schema-registration bug causes attribute traversal `load_config().verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` to raise `AttributeError` — callers in resign.py or compute_validity see an unexpected exception rather than the safe false return.
findings.suggested_fix:
```python
try:
    return load_config().verify.enforce_plan_material_pins
except ConfigError:
    logger.warning("plan_material_pin_config_unreadable", reason="config_error")
    return False
except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001
    logger.warning("plan_material_pin_config_unreadable", reason="unexpected", exc_type=type(exc).__name__)
    return False
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py` (line 242) and `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py` (line 516) as external callers of `resign_plan_review` and confirm their behavior is correct when enforcement-enabled re-sign refusal returns `ok=False` with a pin-health reason.
evidence: `src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py:242`: `return rebar.llm.resign_plan_review(ticket_id)` — outside plan_review/ dir
`src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py:516`: `result = llm.resign_plan_review(args.ticket_id)` — CLI caller also outside plan_review/
Plan does not enumerate either caller in scope discussion or blast-radius acknowledgment
Plan adds a new refusal path (enforcement-enabled + stale pins → ok=False) that callers may need to surface differently
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py` (line 242: `rebar.llm.resign_plan_review(ticket_id)`) is an external caller of `resign_plan_review` outside the `plan_review/` directory. The plan substantially modifies `resign_plan_review` (adds `reviewed_related_material` parsing, pin-health derivation, and enforcement-gated re-sign refusal). This caller is not enumerated anywhere in the plan, and its behavior at the MCP layer (return shape, error handling) may be affected if `resign_plan_review` returns a new shape or raises differently under enforcement.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Edit `resign.py`: `resign_plan_review` … reads `reviewed_related_material`, and invokes `derive_plan_material_pin_health` on those records before signing. With enforcement enabled, stale drift, missing targets, or malformed records refuse re-sign'
scenarios: MCP caller at `_mcp_llm.py` returns a new `ok=False` reason string referencing pin-health to users without any MCP-layer handling being considered
CLI caller at `_llm_commands.py:516` displays a new failure reason without verified formatting

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why reviewed_related_material is stored in the observability sidecar rather than in the signed manifest (alongside verified_at_sha and material:), and document the silent-degradation behavior when the sidecar is absent/pruned.
evidence: sidecar.py docstring (lines 8-9): 'REVIEW_RESULT is NOT in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES, so the reducer skips it (it never enters compiled state, deps, validate, or the close/claim hot paths)'

The plan threads reviewed_related_material through sidecar.emit → sidecar.parse_reviewed_related_material → derive_plan_material_pin_health to enforce re-sign refusals, making a previously advisory, reducer-ignored event load-bearing for security enforcement.

Established alternative not discussed: storing reviewed_related_material in the signed manifest (already done for verified_at_sha, material:, plan-material-pin lines) would be authenticated, tamper-resistant, and aligned with existing manifest security model without conflating observability and enforcement.

The plan explicitly introduces ReviewedRelatedMaterialError for 'any present invalid list/item/key/role/ID/fingerprint/order/duplicate' raised from sidecar parsing — this is enforcement logic, not observability.
findings.finding: The plan uses the observability-only REVIEW_RESULT sidecar as a durable, security-load-bearing baseline for re-sign enforcement (reviewed_related_material), departing from the sidecar's documented invariant ('never enters compiled state, deps, validate, or the close/claim hot paths') without justifying this novel pattern against the established alternative of storing the review-time pin baseline in the signed manifest itself.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'In _run_plan_review … On the full LLM path call sidecar.emit(… reviewed_related_material=review_snapshot.related_material …)' and 'Edit resign.py: resign_plan_review … reads reviewed_related_material, and invokes derive_plan_material_pin_health on those records before signing.'
scenarios: A failed-emit sidecar (crash between verdict and emit) leaves no reviewed_related_material baseline; re-sign then falls through to legacy-unpinned rather than enforcing the pin state actually reviewed, silently weakening the enforcement guarantee.
An operator who prunes sidecars (RETAIN_PER_TICKET=50, but prune is explicit) loses the enforcement baseline for old reviews; re-sign would silently accept legacy-unpinned rather than failing closed.
A future story that makes the sidecar reducer-visible or compactable may inadvertently break or change re-sign enforcement semantics without recognizing the security dependency.
findings.suggested_fix: Consider storing reviewed_related_material as an optional signed manifest field (e.g., 'plan-material-pin-baseline: <serialized>') so it is authenticated, tamper-resistant, and survives sidecar pruning — matching the established pattern used for verified_at_sha and material: fingerprints. If the sidecar approach is retained as deliberate, add an explicit AC covering sidecar-absent re-sign behavior under enforcement and document the departure from the sidecar's reducer-ignored invariant.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
